# Lumen Semantic Search

ALWAYS pass your current working directory as the `cwd` parameter when calling `mcp__lumen__semantic_search`.

ALWAYS use `mcp__lumen__semantic_search` as the FIRST tool for code discovery.

Before using Grep, Glob, Find, or Read for any search, stop and ask:
"Do I already know the exact literal string I'm searching for?"
If not, use `mcp__lumen__semantic_search`.

## Red Flags ‚Äî if you think any of these, STOP:
| Thought | Reality |
|---------|--------|
| "I'll just grep quickly" | `mcp__lumen__semantic_search` is faster for discovery |
| "I know the file name" | You might not know the best match |
| "Glob is faster for this" | Only if you have an exact filename pattern |
| "This is a simple search" | Simple searches benefit most from semantic |

If semantic search is unavailable, Grep/Glob are acceptable fallbacks.
</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>"}}

content.content:
1‚Üí/*!
     2‚ÜíThis crate provides an implementation of line oriented search, with optional
     3‚Üísupport for multi-line search.
     4‚Üí
     5‚Üí# Brief overview
     6‚Üí
     7‚ÜíThe principle type in this crate is a [`Searcher`], which can be configured
     8‚Üíand built by a [`SearcherBuilder`]. A `Searcher` is responsible for reading
     9‚Üíbytes from a source (e.g., a file), executing a search of those bytes using
    10‚Üía `Matcher` (e.g., a regex) and then reporting the results of that search to
    11‚Üía [`Sink`] (e.g., stdout). The `Searcher` itself is principally responsible
    12‚Üífor managing the consumption of bytes from a source and applying a `Matcher`
    13‚Üíover those bytes in an efficient way. The `Searcher` is also responsible for
    14‚Üíinverting a search, counting lines, reporting contextual lines, detecting
    15‚Üíbinary data and even deciding whether or not to use memory maps.
    16‚Üí
    17‚ÜíA `Matcher` (which is defined in the
    18‚Üí[`grep-matcher`](https://crates.io/crates/grep-matcher) crate) is a trait
    19‚Üífor describing the lowest levels of pattern search in a generic way. The
    20‚Üíinterface itself is very similar to the interface of a regular expression.
    21‚ÜíFor example, the [`grep-regex`](https://crates.io/crates/grep-regex)
    22‚Üícrate provides an implementation of the `Matcher` trait using Rust's
    23‚Üí[`regex`](https://crates.io/crates/regex) crate.
    24‚Üí
    25‚ÜíFinally, a `Sink` describes how callers receive search results producer by a
    26‚Üí`Searcher`. This includes routines that are called at the beginning and end of
    27‚Üía search, in addition to routines that are called when matching or contextual
    28‚Üílines are found by the `Searcher`. Implementations of `Sink` can be trivially
    29‚Üísimple, or extraordinarily complex, such as the `Standard` printer found in
    30‚Üíthe [`grep-printer`](https://crates.io/crates/grep-printer) crate, which
    31‚Üíeffectively implements grep-like output. This crate also provides convenience
    32‚Üí`Sink` implementations in the [`sinks`] sub-module for easy searching with
    33‚Üíclosures.
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí# Example
    36‚Üí
    37‚ÜíThis example shows how to execute the searcher and read the search results
    38‚Üíusing the [`UTF8`](sinks::UTF8) implementation of `Sink`.
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí```
    41‚Üíuse {
    42‚Üí    grep_matcher::Matcher,
    43‚Üí    grep_regex::RegexMatcher,
    44‚Üí    grep_searcher::Searcher,
    45‚Üí    grep_searcher::sinks::UTF8,
    46‚Üí};
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üíconst SHERLOCK: &'static [u8] = b"\
    49‚ÜíFor the Doctor Watsons of this world, as opposed to the Sherlock
    50‚ÜíHolmeses, success in the province of detective work must always
    51‚Üíbe, to a very large extent, the result of luck. Sherlock Holmes
    52‚Üícan extract a clew from a wisp of straw or a flake of cigar ash;
    53‚Üíbut Doctor Watson has to have it taken out for him and dusted,
    54‚Üíand exhibited clearly, with a label attached.
    55‚Üí";
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üílet matcher = RegexMatcher::new(r"Doctor \w+")?;
    58‚Üílet mut matches: Vec<(u64, String)> = vec![];
    59‚ÜíSearcher::new().search_slice(&matcher, SHERLOCK, UTF8(|lnum, line| {
    60‚Üí    // We are guaranteed to find a match, so the unwrap is OK.
    61‚Üí    let mymatch = matcher.find(line.as_bytes())?.unwrap();
    62‚Üí    matches.push((lnum, line[mymatch].to_string()));
    63‚Üí    Ok(true)
    64‚Üí}))?;
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üíassert_eq!(matches.len(), 2);
    67‚Üíassert_eq!(
    68‚Üí    matches[0],
    69‚Üí    (1, "Doctor Watsons".to_string())
    70‚Üí);
    71‚Üíassert_eq!(
    72‚Üí    matches[1],
    73‚Üí    (5, "Doctor Watson".to_string())
    74‚Üí);
    75‚Üí
    76‚Üí# Ok::<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>(())
    77‚Üí```
    78‚Üí
    79‚ÜíSee also `examples/search-stdin.rs` from the root of this crate's directory
    80‚Üíto see a similar example that accepts a pattern on the command line and
    81‚Üísearches stdin.
    82‚Üí*/
    83‚Üí
    84‚Üí#![deny(missing_docs)]
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üípub use crate::{
    87‚Üí    lines::{LineIter, LineStep},
    88‚Üí    searcher::{
    89‚Üí        BinaryDetection, ConfigError, Encoding, MmapChoice, Searcher,
    90‚Üí        SearcherBuilder,
    91‚Üí    },
    92‚Üí    sink::{
    93‚Üí        Sink, SinkContext, SinkContextKind, SinkError, SinkFinish, SinkMatch,
    94‚Üí        sinks,
    95‚Üí    },
    96‚Üí};
    97‚Üí
    98‚Üí#[macro_use]
    99‚Üímod macros;
   100‚Üí
   101‚Üímod line_buffer;
   102‚Üímod lines;
   103‚Üímod searcher;
   104‚Üímod sink;
   105‚Üí#[cfg(test)]
   106‚Üímod testutil;
   107‚Üí

<system-reminder>

content.content:
1‚Üí/*!
     2‚ÜíThis crate provides an interface for regular expressions, with a focus on line
     3‚Üíoriented search. The purpose of this crate is to provide a low level matching
     4‚Üíinterface that permits any kind of substring or regex implementation to power
     5‚Üíthe search routines provided by the
     6‚Üí[`grep-searcher`](https://docs.rs/grep-searcher)
     7‚Üícrate.
     8‚Üí
     9‚ÜíThe primary thing provided by this crate is the [`Matcher`] trait. The trait
    10‚Üídefines an abstract interface for text search. It is robust enough to support
    11‚Üíeverything from basic substring search all the way to arbitrarily complex
    12‚Üíregular expression implementations without sacrificing performance.
    13‚Üí
    14‚ÜíA key design decision made in this crate is the use of *internal iteration*,
    15‚Üíor otherwise known as the "push" model of searching. In this paradigm,
    16‚Üíimplementations of the `Matcher` trait will drive search and execute callbacks
    17‚Üíprovided by the caller when a match is found. This is in contrast to the
    18‚Üíusual style of *external iteration* (the "pull" model) found throughout the
    19‚ÜíRust ecosystem. There are two primary reasons why internal iteration was
    20‚Üíchosen:
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí* Some search implementations may themselves require internal iteration.
    23‚Üí  Converting an internal iterator to an external iterator can be non-trivial
    24‚Üí  and sometimes even practically impossible.
    25‚Üí* Rust's type system isn't quite expressive enough to write a generic interface
    26‚Üí  using external iteration without giving something else up (namely, ease of
    27‚Üí  use and/or performance).
    28‚Üí
    29‚ÜíIn other words, internal iteration was chosen because it is the lowest common
    30‚Üídenominator and because it is probably the least bad way of expressing the
    31‚Üíinterface in today's Rust. As a result, this trait isn't specifically intended
    32‚Üífor everyday use, although, you might find it to be a happy price to pay if you
    33‚Üíwant to write code that is generic over multiple different regex
    34‚Üíimplementations.
    35‚Üí*/
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí#![deny(missing_docs)]
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üíuse crate::interpolate::interpolate;
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üímod interpolate;
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí/// The type of a match.
    44‚Üí///
    45‚Üí/// The type of a match is a possibly empty range pointing to a contiguous
    46‚Üí/// block of addressable memory.
    47‚Üí///
    48‚Üí/// Every `Match` is guaranteed to satisfy the invariant that `start <= end`.
    49‚Üí///
    50‚Üí/// # Indexing
    51‚Üí///
    52‚Üí/// This type is structurally identical to `std::ops::Range<usize>`, but
    53‚Üí/// is a bit more ergonomic for dealing with match indices. In particular,
    54‚Üí/// this type implements `Copy` and provides methods for building new `Match`
    55‚Üí/// values based on old `Match` values. Finally, the invariant that `start`
    56‚Üí/// is always less than or equal to `end` is enforced.
    57‚Üí///
    58‚Üí/// A `Match` can be used to slice a `&[u8]`, `&mut [u8]` or `&str` using
    59‚Üí/// range notation. e.g.,
    60‚Üí///
    61‚Üí/// ```
    62‚Üí/// use grep_matcher::Match;
    63‚Üí///
    64‚Üí/// let m = Match::new(2, 5);
    65‚Üí/// let bytes = b"abcdefghi";
    66‚Üí/// assert_eq!(b"cde", &bytes[m]);
    67‚Üí/// ```
    68‚Üí#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Eq, Hash, PartialEq)]
    69‚Üípub struct Match {
    70‚Üí    start: usize,
    71‚Üí    end: usize,
    72‚Üí}
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üíimpl Match {
    75‚Üí    /// Create a new match.
    76‚Üí    ///
    77‚Üí    /// # Panics
    78‚Üí    ///
    79‚Üí    /// This function panics if `start > end`.
    80‚Üí    #[inline]
    81‚Üí    pub fn new(start: usize, end: usize) -> Match {
    82‚Üí        assert!(start <= end);
    83‚Üí        Match { start, end }
    84‚Üí    }
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üí    /// Creates a zero width match at the given offset.
    87‚Üí    #[inline]
    88‚Üí    pub fn zero(offset: usize) -> Match {
    89‚Üí        Match { start: offset, end: offset }
    90‚Üí    }
    91‚Üí
    92‚Üí    /// Return the start offset of this match.
    93‚Üí    #[inline]
    94‚Üí    pub fn start(&self) -> usize {
    95‚Üí        self.start
    96‚Üí    }
    97‚Üí
    98‚Üí    /// Return the end offset of this match.
    99‚Üí    #[inline]
   100‚Üí    pub fn end(&self) -> usize {

<system-reminder>

1‚Üí/*!
     2‚ÜíThis crate provides featureful and fast printers that interoperate with the
     3‚Üí[`grep-searcher`](https://docs.rs/grep-searcher)
     4‚Üícrate.
     5‚Üí
     6‚Üí# Brief overview
     7‚Üí
     8‚ÜíThe [`Standard`] printer shows results in a human readable format, and is
     9‚Üímodeled after the formats used by standard grep-like tools. Features include,
    10‚Üíbut are not limited to, cross platform terminal coloring, search & replace,
    11‚Üímulti-line result handling and reporting summary statistics.
    12‚Üí
    13‚ÜíThe [`JSON`] printer shows results in a machine readable format.
    14‚ÜíTo facilitate a stream of search results, the format uses [JSON
    15‚ÜíLines](https://jsonlines.org/) by emitting a series of messages as search
    16‚Üíresults are found.
    17‚Üí
    18‚ÜíThe [`Summary`] printer shows *aggregate* results for a single search in a
    19‚Üíhuman readable format, and is modeled after similar formats found in standard
    20‚Üígrep-like tools. This printer is useful for showing the total number of matches
    21‚Üíand/or printing file paths that either contain or don't contain matches.
    22‚Üí
    23‚Üí# Example
    24‚Üí
    25‚ÜíThis example shows how to create a "standard" printer and execute a search.
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí```
    28‚Üíuse {
    29‚Üí    grep_regex::RegexMatcher,
    30‚Üí    grep_printer::Standard,
    31‚Üí    grep_searcher::Searcher,
    32‚Üí};
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üíconst SHERLOCK: &'static [u8] = b"\
    35‚ÜíFor the Doctor Watsons of this world, as opposed to the Sherlock
    36‚ÜíHolmeses, success in the province of detective work must always
    37‚Üíbe, to a very large extent, the result of luck. Sherlock Holmes
    38‚Üícan extract a clew from a wisp of straw or a flake of cigar ash;
    39‚Üíbut Doctor Watson has to have it taken out for him and dusted,
    40‚Üíand exhibited clearly, with a label attached.
    41‚Üí";
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üílet matcher = RegexMatcher::new(r"Sherlock")?;
    44‚Üílet mut printer = Standard::new_no_color(vec![]);
    45‚ÜíSearcher::new().search_slice(&matcher, SHERLOCK, printer.sink(&matcher))?;
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí// into_inner gives us back the underlying writer we provided to
    48‚Üí// new_no_color, which is wrapped in a termcolor::NoColor. Thus, a second
    49‚Üí// into_inner gives us back the actual buffer.
    50‚Üílet output = String::from_utf8(printer.into_inner().into_inner())?;
    51‚Üílet expected = "\
    52‚Üí1:For the Doctor Watsons of this world, as opposed to the Sherlock
    53‚Üí3:be, to a very large extent, the result of luck. Sherlock Holmes
    54‚Üí";
    55‚Üíassert_eq!(output, expected);
    56‚Üí# Ok::<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>(())
    57‚Üí```
    58‚Üí*/
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí#![deny(missing_docs)]
    61‚Üí#![cfg_attr(docsrs, feature(doc_cfg))]
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üípub use crate::{
    64‚Üí    color::{ColorError, ColorSpecs, UserColorSpec, default_color_specs},
    65‚Üí    hyperlink::{
    66‚Üí        HyperlinkAlias, HyperlinkConfig, HyperlinkEnvironment,
    67‚Üí        HyperlinkFormat, HyperlinkFormatError, hyperlink_aliases,
    68‚Üí    },
    69‚Üí    path::{PathPrinter, PathPrinterBuilder},
    70‚Üí    standard::{Standard, StandardBuilder, StandardSink},
    71‚Üí    stats::Stats,
    72‚Üí    summary::{Summary, SummaryBuilder, SummaryKind, SummarySink},
    73‚Üí};
    74‚Üí
    75‚Üí#[cfg(feature = "serde")]
    76‚Üípub use crate::json::{JSON, JSONBuilder, JSONSink};
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üí// The maximum number of bytes to execute a search to account for look-ahead.
    79‚Üí//
    80‚Üí// This is an unfortunate kludge since PCRE2 doesn't provide a way to search
    81‚Üí// a substring of some input while accounting for look-ahead. In theory, we
    82‚Üí// could refactor the various 'grep' interfaces to account for it, but it would
    83‚Üí// be a large change. So for now, we just let PCRE2 go looking a bit for a
    84‚Üí// match without searching the entire rest of the contents.
    85‚Üí//
    86‚Üí// Note that this kludge is only active in multi-line mode.
    87‚Üíconst MAX_LOOK_AHEAD: usize = 128;
    88‚Üí
    89‚Üí#[macro_use]
    90‚Üímod macros;
    91‚Üí
    92‚Üímod color;

file.content:
/*!
This crate provides common routines used in command line applications, with a
focus on routines useful for search oriented applications. As a utility
library, there is no central type or function. However, a key focus of this
crate is to improve failure modes and provide user friendly error messages
when things go wrong.

To the best extent possible, everything in this crate works on Windows, macOS
and Linux.


# Standard I/O

[`is_readable_stdin`] determines whether stdin can be usefully read from. It
is useful when writing an application that changes behavior based on whether
the application was invoked with data on stdin. For example, `rg foo` might
recursively search the current working directory for occurrences of `foo`, but
`rg foo < file` might only search the contents of `file`.


# Coloring and buffering

The [`stdout`], [`stdout_buffered_block`] and [`stdout_buffered_line`] routines
are alternative constructors for [`StandardStream`]. A `StandardStream`
implements `termcolor::WriteColor`, which provides a way to emit colors to
terminals. Its key use is the encapsulation of buffering style. Namely,
`stdout` will return a line buffered `StandardStream` if and only if
stdout is connected to a tty, and will otherwise return a block buffered
`StandardStream`. Line buffering is important for use with a tty because it
typically decreases the latency at which the end user sees output. Block
buffering is used otherwise because it is faster, and redirecting stdout to a
file typically doesn't benefit from the decreased latency that line buffering
provides.

The `stdout_buffered_block` and `stdout_buffered_line` can be used to
explicitly set the buffering strategy regardless of whether stdout is connected
to a tty or not.


# Escaping

The [`escape`](crate::escape()), [`escape_os`], [`unescape`] and
[`unescape_os`] routines provide a user friendly way of dealing with UTF-8
encoded strings that can express arbitrary bytes. For example, you might want
to accept a string containing arbitrary bytes as a command line argument, but
most interactive shells make such strings difficult to type. Instead, we can
ask users to use escape sequences.

For example, `a\xFFz` is itself a valid UTF-8 string corresponding to the
following bytes:

```ignore
[b'a', b'\\', b'x', b'F', b'F', b'z']
```

However, we can
interpret `\xFF` as an escape sequence with the `unescape`/`unescape_os`
routines, which will yield

```ignore
[b'a', b'\xFF', b'z']
```

instead. For example:

```
use grep_cli::unescape;

// Note the use of a raw string!
assert_eq!(vec![b'a', b'\xFF', b'z'], unescape(r"a\xFFz"));
```

The `escape`/`escape_os` routines provide the reverse transformation, which
makes it easy to show user friendly error messages involving arbitrary bytes.


# Building patterns

Typically, regular expression patterns must be valid UTF-8. However, command
line arguments aren't guaranteed to be valid UTF-8. Unfortunately, the standard

1‚Üí/*!
     2‚ÜíThe ignore crate provides a fast recursive directory iterator that respects
     3‚Üívarious filters such as globs, file types and `.gitignore` files. The precise
     4‚Üímatching rules and precedence is explained in the documentation for
     5‚Üí`WalkBuilder`.
     6‚Üí
     7‚ÜíSecondarily, this crate exposes gitignore and file type matchers for use cases
     8‚Üíthat demand more fine-grained control.
     9‚Üí
    10‚Üí# Example
    11‚Üí
    12‚ÜíThis example shows the most basic usage of this crate. This code will
    13‚Üírecursively traverse the current directory while automatically filtering out
    14‚Üífiles and directories according to ignore globs found in files like
    15‚Üí`.ignore` and `.gitignore`:
    16‚Üí
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí```rust,no_run
    19‚Üíuse ignore::Walk;
    20‚Üí
    21‚Üífor result in Walk::new("./") {
    22‚Üí    // Each item yielded by the iterator is either a directory entry or an
    23‚Üí    // error, so either print the path or the error.
    24‚Üí    match result {
    25‚Üí        Ok(entry) => println!("{}", entry.path().display()),
    26‚Üí        Err(err) => println!("ERROR: {}", err),
    27‚Üí    }
    28‚Üí}
    29‚Üí```
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí# Example: advanced
    32‚Üí
    33‚ÜíBy default, the recursive directory iterator will ignore hidden files and
    34‚Üídirectories. This can be disabled by building the iterator with `WalkBuilder`:
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí```rust,no_run
    37‚Üíuse ignore::WalkBuilder;
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üífor result in WalkBuilder::new("./").hidden(false).build() {
    40‚Üí    println!("{:?}", result);
    41‚Üí}
    42‚Üí```
    43‚Üí
    44‚ÜíSee the documentation for `WalkBuilder` for many other options.
    45‚Üí*/
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí#![deny(missing_docs)]
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üíuse std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üípub use crate::walk::{
    52‚Üí    DirEntry, ParallelVisitor, ParallelVisitorBuilder, Walk, WalkBuilder,
    53‚Üí    WalkParallel, WalkState,
    54‚Üí};
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üímod default_types;
    57‚Üímod dir;
    58‚Üípub mod gitignore;
    59‚Üípub mod overrides;
    60‚Üímod pathutil;
    61‚Üípub mod types;
    62‚Üímod walk;
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí/// Represents an error that can occur when parsing a gitignore file.
    65‚Üí#[derive(Debug)]
    66‚Üípub enum Error {
    67‚Üí    /// A collection of "soft" errors. These occur when adding an ignore
    68‚Üí    /// file partially succeeded.
    69‚Üí    Partial(Vec<Error>),
    70‚Üí    /// An error associated with a specific line number.
    71‚Üí    WithLineNumber {
    72‚Üí        /// The line number.
    73‚Üí        line: u64,
    74‚Üí        /// The underlying error.
    75‚Üí        err: Box<Error>,
    76‚Üí    },
    77‚Üí    /// An error associated with a particular file path.
    78‚Üí    WithPath {
    79‚Üí        /// The file path.
    80‚Üí        path: PathBuf,
